claims.claim: It computes estimated token savings, break-even downstream processing throughput, and net latency savings under a configurable prefill tokens-per-second assumption.

evidence_refs.quote: # Compression Overhead Estimator: A Local Estimator for Byte-Compression Break-Even Analysis in LLM Prefill Pipelines

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: On an aarch64 host with 20 CPUs, compressible 1 MiB synthetic corpora yielded net latency savings of approximately 45–52 seconds at a 5,000 tokens/s downstream assumption, with break-even throughputs ranging from approximately 4.59M to 285M estimated tokens/s—well above typical LLM prefill rates.

claims.claim: Random (incompressible) payloads correctly produced no latency win.

evidence_refs.quote: We present a lightweight, locally runnable estimator that quantifies when byte-level compression overhead is worth paying in pipelines where compressed data bypasses downstream token processing. The estimator measures compression and decompression wall-clock latency, throughput, and compression ratio for synthetic text, code, JSON, and random payloads using standard-library compressors (zlib, bz2, lzma). claims.claim: These results are bounded: synthetic corpora are not representative of production workloads, the estimator does not score semantic compression quality, and the 4 bytes/token heuristic requires replacement with a target-model tokenizer for deployment decisions.
